subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
#### Boards and Wheels
- **1 production wheel** — Central mechanism that tracks resource availability. Goods become more plentiful over time.
- **Game board strips** — Landscape strips forming each player's territory.
- **Building cards** — Various buildings that can be constructed and used.

#### Resources
- **Basic goods** — Wood, clay, peat, grain, livestock, coins.
- **Processed goods** — Flour, bread, wine, beer, whiskey, meat, ceramic, bricks, straw, ornaments, books, relics.
- **Energy** — Peat, wood, and coal serve as energy for processing.

#### Tiles
- **Landscape tiles** — Forest, moor, coastal, hillside, and plains. Expand your territory.
- **Building tiles** — Cloisters, settlements, and various production buildings.
- **Settlement tiles** — Required for settlement formation scoring.

#### Other Components
- **Prior and lay brother tokens** — Workers for each player.
- **Clergy markers** — Placed on buildings to use them.
- **Round marker** — Tracks game progress through 24/25 rounds (depending on variant).

subtopics.title: Board Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Production Wheel Setup
1. **Assemble the production wheel** — Place the wheel in the center of the table. The wheel has a rotating arm that advances each round.
2. **Set starting positions** — Place goods markers on the wheel at their starting values. As the wheel turns, each good's production value increases.
3. **Goods tracks** — Each good has a track on the wheel showing how many of that resource are generated when you take the production action.

#### Landscape Setup
1. **Starting landscape** — Each player begins with a heartland strip containing a few basic terrain tiles.
2. **Available landscapes** — Place additional landscape tiles in a supply sorted by type.
3. **Building display** — Sort available buildings by cost and place them where all players can see.

#### Variant Selection
- **France variant** — Focuses on wine production and cloisters.
- **Ireland variant** — Focuses on whiskey production and different building set.
- **Choose one** — Players agree on France or Ireland before setup, as building sets differ.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Starting Components
- **Heartland** — Each player takes their starting landscape strip.
- **Clergy** — Each player receives 1 Prior and 2 Lay Brothers.
- **Starting resources** — Players receive a small starting allocation of basic goods (varies slightly by turn order).
- **Coin** — Starting player gets fewer coins; later players get compensation coins.

#### Territory
- **Landscape grid** — Your territory is arranged in a grid pattern. Buildings and landscapes are placed in this grid.
- **Expansion** — Throughout the game you purchase additional landscape strips to expand your territory.

subtopics.title: Game Overview
subtopics.content:
#### Core Concept
Ora et Labora ("Pray and Work") is a resource management and building game themed around medieval monastery life. Players manage a monastic estate, producing goods, constructing buildings, and forming settlements. The unique production wheel mechanism means goods become more plentiful as the game progresses, creating natural pacing.

#### Game Arc
- **24-25 rounds** — Game length depends on variant (France vs. Ireland).
- **Settlement scoring** — At fixed intervals, players score their settlements (groups of connected buildings meeting specific criteria).
- **Escalating power** — Buildings become more powerful and expensive over time.
- **Territory growth** — Players expand their landscape grids throughout the game.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Round Structure
1. **Advance the wheel** — At the start of each round, rotate the production wheel one notch. This increases the production value of all goods by 1.
2. **Active player's turn** — The active player takes exactly ONE action.
3. **Pass to next player** — Play passes clockwise.

#### Available Actions (Choose One)
1. **Fell trees / Cut peat** — Remove a forest or moor tile from your landscape to gain wood or peat (amount based on production wheel).
2. **Use a building** — Place one of your clergy members on a building to activate it. You can use your own buildings for free, or pay to use opponents' buildings.
3. **Build a building** — Pay the resource cost to construct a building and place it on an empty space in your landscape.
4. **Buy a landscape** — Pay coins to acquire a new landscape strip and attach it to your territory.

#### Clergy Placement
- **Prior** — Your Prior can be placed on any building (your own or opponents').
- **Lay Brothers** — Lay Brothers can only be placed on your own buildings.
- **Recall** — After all your clergy are placed, you must recall all of them before placing any again. This creates a natural pacing to building use.
- **Using opponents' buildings** — You must pay the owner a usage fee to use their building with your Prior.

subtopics.title: Key Actions
subtopics.content:
#### Production Wheel Mechanism
- **Accumulation** — Each good on the wheel increases by 1 each round that no one takes it.
- **Taking production** — When you use a production building, you take goods equal to the current wheel value for that resource, then reset it to 0.
- **Timing** — Waiting longer gives more goods, but opponents may take them first.

#### Building Construction
- **Cost** — Buildings cost combinations of resources (wood, clay, stone, coins, etc.).
- **Placement** — Buildings must be placed on valid terrain in your landscape grid.
- **Adjacency** — Some buildings have placement requirements regarding adjacent tiles.
- **Building types** — Production buildings, processing buildings, civic buildings, and religious buildings (cloisters).

#### Processing Chains
- **Grain chain** — Grain → Flour (Grain Mill) → Bread (Bakery, requires energy).
- **Livestock chain** — Livestock → Meat (Slaughterhouse, requires energy).
- **Clay chain** — Clay → Ceramic or Bricks (Pottery, requires energy).
- **Wood chain** — Wood serves as building material and energy source.
- **France: Wine** — Grapes → Wine (Winery). Wine is very valuable.
- **Ireland: Whiskey** — Grain → Whiskey (Distillery). Whiskey is very valuable.

#### Landscape Expansion
- **Purchase** — Buy landscape strips with coins during your turn.
- **Coastal, hillside, plains** — Each type provides different terrain for buildings.
- **Strategic value** — More territory means more room for buildings and settlements.

subtopics.title: Special Rules
subtopics.content:
#### Settlement Scoring
- **Settlement formation** — A settlement is a connected group of buildings that includes at least one house/dwelling.
- **Scoring triggers** — Settlements are scored at fixed points during the game (after specific rounds).
- **Settlement requirements** — Each settlement must contain specific building types to qualify for scoring.
- **Multiple settlements** — You can form multiple settlements in your territory.

#### Cloisters
- **Religious buildings** — Cloisters are special buildings that score bonus points and provide powerful abilities.
- **Cloister adjacency** — Cloisters score bonuses based on the buildings adjacent to them.
- **Variant differences** — France and Ireland have different cloister buildings.

#### France vs. Ireland
- **France** — Features wine production, different cloister set, emphasis on ornamental goods.
- **Ireland** — Features whiskey production, different building mix, slightly different scoring emphasis.
- **Core rules identical** — The production wheel, clergy, and landscape mechanics work the same in both.

#### Coins
- **Currency** — Coins are used to buy landscapes, pay building usage fees, and occasionally as building costs.
- **Income** — Some buildings generate coins when used.
- **Not VP** — Coins do not count directly as victory points.

subtopics.title: Scoring and Endgame
subtopics.content:
#### Game End
- **Final round** — After the last round (24 or 25), the game ends.
- **Final settlement scoring** — One last settlement scoring phase occurs.

#### Scoring Categories
- **Settlement values** — Points from all settlement scoring phases throughout the game.
- **Building values** — Each building in your territory has a printed VP value.
- **Goods conversion** — Certain high-value goods (wine, whiskey, relics, books, ornaments) can be converted to VP at specific rates.
- **Unsettled buildings** — Buildings not part of any settlement may score reduced or no points.

#### Winner
- **Highest total** — Player with the most victory points wins.
- **Tiebreaker** — The player with more remaining goods wins ties.

subtopics.title: Beginner Tips
subtopics.content:
- **Watch the production wheel** — Timing when to take goods is crucial. Letting values build up is good, but don't let opponents take a huge haul.
- **Expand your landscape early** — More territory means more room for buildings and settlements.
- **Plan your clergy carefully** — You only have 3 clergy members. Don't place them all quickly without a plan for recall.
- **Build settlements incrementally** — Don't wait until the last scoring round. Score at every opportunity.
- **Process your goods** — Raw goods are worth little. Converting grain to bread or clay to bricks multiplies their value.

subtopics.title: Key Strategies
subtopics.content:
#### Strategy 1: Settlement Focus
- **Goal** — Maximize settlement scoring by building dense clusters of buildings.
- **Key actions** — Build houses early, cluster buildings for adjacency, score at every opportunity.

#### Strategy 2: Production Engine
- **Goal** — Build an efficient processing chain to generate high-value goods.
- **Key actions** — Focus on grain/wine/whiskey production buildings. Time wheel takes for maximum output.

#### Strategy 3: Cloister Network
- **Goal** — Build multiple cloisters with strong adjacency bonuses.
- **Key actions** — Plan landscape layout to maximize cloister adjacencies. Religious buildings often have high VP values.

#### General Principles
- **Clergy management** — Having your clergy recalled at the right time (when you need to act) is critical. Don't waste placements.
- **Deny opponents** — Using an opponent's building costs you money but denies them the action.
- **Watch scoring rounds** — Know when settlement scoring occurs and prepare accordingly.
